summary: Big news in Reynoldstown where that long-empty corner near the Beltline is finally seeing construction after five years. Meanwhile, Atlanta's airport chaos continues with ICE agents walking around but mostly just standing there while TSA lines stretch for hours.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Empty Beltline Corner Finally Sees Construction After 5 Years
topStory.summary: That cleared lot at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown is finally getting built on after sitting empty since 2021. Embry Development is moving forward with 85 townhomes and condos about a block from the Eastside Trail. It's a sign that even delayed projects are moving again near the Beltline, though neighbors have been waiting a long time for something to happen on that prominent corner.
topStory.body:
If you've walked the Eastside Trail recently, you know that cleared corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown. It's been an empty reminder of stalled development for nearly five years now, ever since the original buildings were demolished in 2021. But finally, there's movement.

Embry Development Company, which bought the 2-acre site from Toll Brothers two years ago, has started groundwork for what will become Camber Crossing. The project calls for 85 residential units, a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos, just a block west of the Beltline's Eastside Trail. You know the spot - it's right there where Memorial Drive meets all that foot traffic heading to Ponce City Market.

This is exactly the kind of infill development the Beltline was supposed to encourage, though the timeline shows how even prime real estate can sit idle when financing gets tricky. The original developer got permits but couldn't make the numbers work. Now Embry is betting that the market has caught up to the location, and honestly, with everything else happening along the trail, it's probably the right call.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Reynoldstown development site near the Beltline

stories.headline: Decatur's 'Missing Middle' Housing Village Almost Ready to Launch
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is wrapping up construction on 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards on Midway Road. The goal is creating more accessible single-family housing near Decatur, where median home values have hit $700,000. It's the kind of village-style development that could offer a middle ground between apartments and expensive houses.
stories.body:
With Decatur home prices pushing $700,000, finding affordable single-family housing ITP feels nearly impossible. But Cottages at Midway might offer a solution that's been missing from the market. The 18-house development on Midway Road is designed as a village-style community with shared courtyards and green space, replacing two underused lots.

The project is targeting what planners call "missing middle" housing - the space between apartments and expensive single-family homes. Instead of maximizing density, the developers focused on community design, clustering houses around common areas that encourage neighbors to actually know each other. It's a throwback to how neighborhoods used to be built.

A soft launch starts next week to drum up interest before the full opening. The location puts residents within walking distance of downtown Decatur while staying at price points that might actually work for middle-class families. If it succeeds, expect more developers to try similar approaches to the affordability crisis.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan for Cottages at Midway development in Decatur

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant today on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. The menu features Peking chicken platters, soft shell crab bao, and Thai tea martinis. Both are restaurant industry veterans - Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in NYC before moving to Atlanta.
stories.body:
After years of pop-ups and restaurant industry experience, chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez finally have their own place. Mushi Ni opened today on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park, right by the Freedom Park Trail, serving what they call Asian-American comfort food.

The menu hits that sweet spot between familiar and interesting. You'll find soft shell crab bao and chicken longanisa alongside umami fries and crab Rangoon puffs. The Peking chicken platter feeds two for $25, which feels reasonable for the neighborhood. They're also doing Thai tea martinis and other cocktails for both lunch and dinner.

Jimenez brings serious credentials - she worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta. Le has his own restaurant background. Together, they've been testing concepts around the city, and the Inman Park location gives them a chance to build something permanent. The Elizabeth Street spot puts them right in the path of trail traffic, which should help with discovery.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish from Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style BBQ to Ansley Mall
stories.summary: John Lewis, the pitmaster who helped put Austin barbecue on the map, has opened at Ansley Mall behind The Cook's Warehouse. The upstairs smokehouse has panoramic windows overlooking the new Beltline pedestrian bridge, with six 22-foot smokers running 24 hours a day. It's some of the best brisket you'll find in Atlanta.
stories.body:
The smokehouse at Lewis Barbecue is unlike anything you've seen in Atlanta. Most barbecue joints hide their cookers in back rooms or outdoor sheds, but John Lewis put his six 22-foot smokers upstairs in a loft with floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking the new Beltline pedestrian bridge. It looks like the fanciest engine room in Georgia, and it runs 24 hours a day.

Lewis helped weld those smokers himself out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks. The man knows his way around both Austin and Charleston barbecue scenes, and he's brought that knowledge to Ansley Mall. The brisket here competes with anything you'll find in the city, with that perfect balance of smoke and heat that makes great Texas-style barbecue.

The location behind The Cook's Warehouse puts Lewis right on the Beltline corridor, catching foot traffic from the trail. The timing feels right - Atlanta's barbecue scene has room for someone with Lewis's credentials, and the Ansley Mall spot gives him the space to do things properly. Those panoramic windows aren't just for show; they let you watch the whole smoking process while you eat.
stories.imageAlt: Barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue with meat and sides

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's New Food Hall Opens in April Near East Lake
stories.summary: The creator of NYC's Gansevoort Market is opening Vela Park Food Works near East Lake Golf Club in mid-April. It's his third Atlanta food hall, featuring six chef-driven concepts with craft cocktails and outdoor dining. The food hall anchors the new Vela Park apartment community.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid, the mastermind behind New York City's famous Gansevoort Market, is bringing his food hall expertise to Atlanta's eastside. Vela Park Food Works is finishing construction now at the new Vela Park apartment community near East Lake Golf Club, with an opening planned for mid-April.

This marks Montwaid's third multi-concept food destination in Atlanta, building on his track record of creating spaces that work for both residents and destination diners. The food hall will feature six different chef-driven concepts, along with craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor dining space. The setup mirrors what made Gansevoort Market so successful - multiple options in one space with room to spread out.

The location near East Lake puts it in a part of town that's been waiting for this kind of dining destination. The apartment community gives it a built-in customer base, but the food hall concept should draw from across the eastside. Montwaid has proven he knows how to make these projects work, and the April opening gives him a good runway into summer when outdoor dining really picks up.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing dining areas

stories.headline: ICE Agents Walk Around Atlanta Airport While TSA Lines Stretch for Hours
stories.summary: Federal immigration agents are at Hartsfield-Jackson to help with security as the government shutdown continues, but travelers say they're mostly just standing around. TSA agents are working without pay, leading to long lines and unpredictable wait times. The airport warned of four-hour delays on Monday.
stories.body:
The scene at Hartsfield-Jackson this week has been something else. ICE agents in tactical gear are walking around the terminals, supposedly helping with security as TSA agents work without pay during the partial government shutdown. But travelers are telling a different story - the agents are mostly just standing there, not really doing anything to help the lines move faster.

Mayor Dickens assured everyone that the ICE agents won't be doing immigration enforcement during this deployment, just providing security support. Still, it's an unusual sight at the world's busiest airport, and it highlights how desperate the situation has become. TSA agents have been working without paychecks for over a month, and nearly 3,500 called out on Monday alone.

The wait times have been all over the place - four hours one day, 30 minutes the next. Delta even suspended its special services for Congress members because of resource constraints. It's a mess that's affecting everyone trying to travel through Atlanta, and there's no clear end in sight until the shutdown gets resolved.
stories.imageAlt: Long TSA security lines at Atlanta airport during government shutdown

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Project Almost Done After Years of Planning
stories.summary: The West Howard Avenue project between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA station is wrapping up after nearly a decade in planning. The one-mile stretch now has a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, wider sidewalks, and traffic calming measures. It transforms what used to be a high-speed corridor into something more people-friendly.
stories.body:
After nearly a decade of planning, Decatur's big Complete Streets project on West Howard Avenue is almost finished. The roughly one-mile stretch between Commerce Drive (near Decatur High School) and Paden Circle (by East Lake MARTA station) now looks completely different from the high-speed drag strip it used to be.

The centerpiece is a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers running most of the length, along with wider sidewalks and traffic calming measures throughout. It's the kind of infrastructure that makes streets work for people, not just cars. The project connects downtown Decatur to the MARTA station, which should encourage more people to bike or walk instead of driving.

Work started last August, and you can see the transformation is nearly complete. The concrete barriers and repaved sections make it clear this isn't just a paint job - it's a fundamental rethinking of how this corridor functions. Other cities are watching projects like this to see if Complete Streets really work in practice, and Decatur's version should provide some good lessons.
stories.imageAlt: New bike lanes and sidewalks on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls as Firefighters Blast Mayor
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced resolutions to bar ICE detention facilities in Atlanta and require documentation when agents work with police. Meanwhile, dozens of firefighters criticized Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign their first-ever union contract that council already ratified.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council's Public Safety Committee had a lot to unpack this week, and most of it centered on federal enforcement and labor disputes. The committee unanimously advanced two resolutions targeting ICE operations - one would ban detention facilities within city limits, while the other requires Atlanta police to document any time ICE agents are present during their operations.

But the bigger drama came from dozens of firefighters who showed up to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign their union contract. The city council ratified the agreement back in April 2025, making it Atlanta's first-ever firefighter union contract, but Dickens has been sitting on it ever since. The timing is particularly awkward given all the federal agents currently walking around the airport.

Both ICE resolutions head to the full council for a vote on April 20. The firefighter contract situation remains in limbo, with union members clearly frustrated that their groundbreaking agreement is stuck in the mayor's office while other cities are moving ahead with similar deals.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta firefighters at city council meeting

stories.headline: 15-Year-Old Arrested After Gun Found at Hillgrove High School
stories.summary: A weapon detection system at Hillgrove High School found a gun in a student's possession, triggering a Code Red lockdown. The 15-year-old was arrested within 12 minutes, but the incident left parents and students shaken. The school's principal credited the detection technology for preventing a potentially dangerous situation.
stories.body:
Hillgrove High School went into Code Red lockdown Tuesday morning after a weapon detection system found a gun on a 15-year-old student. The entire incident, from detection to arrest, was resolved in about 12 minutes, according to Cobb County school officials. Nobody was injured, but those 12 minutes felt much longer for parents and students.

The weapon was reportedly found in the cafeteria, and the school's detection technology immediately alerted authorities. This is exactly the kind of situation these systems are designed for - catching weapons before they can be used. The principal specifically credited the detection equipment for the quick response, which prevented what could have been a much more serious incident.

Parents lined up outside the school during the lockdown, and students were understandably shaken by the whole experience. It's become an unfortunately familiar scene at schools across Georgia, but the quick resolution shows that security measures can work when they're properly implemented. The question is whether we should be relieved that the system worked or concerned that we need these systems in the first place.
stories.imageAlt: Police response at Hillgrove High School during lockdown

stories.headline: 'Atlanta-Conda': 8-Foot Python Found on the Beltline
stories.summary: Video captured someone handling an 8- to 10-foot python found along the Atlanta Beltline, but wildlife officials are warning people not to handle snakes they find. It's definitely not a typical Georgia snake, and experts are concerned about people trying to grab unknown reptiles.
stories.body:
Just when you think you've seen everything on the Beltline, someone finds an 8-foot python. Joseph Ragland was walking the trail when he spotted what he calls the "Atlanta-Conda" and started filming. The video shows someone's uncle (apparently) yanking the snake out of some bushes like it's no big deal, leaving 10 feet of snake sprawled across the trail.

Ragland has no idea if the person handling the snake is the owner or just someone who decided to help. Either way, officials from Southeastern Reptile Rescue are not happy about it. Jason Clark says the video is concerning because handling unknown snakes, especially large ones, can be dangerous for both the person and the animal.

This definitely wasn't a typical Georgia snake - pythons aren't native here, so it was either someone's escaped pet or an abandoned animal. The Beltline attracts all kinds of wildlife, but usually we're talking about birds and the occasional fox, not massive constrictors. Wildlife officials want people to call professionals instead of trying to wrestle snakes themselves.
stories.imageAlt: Large python on the Atlanta Beltline being handled by person
